title: Scaling Your Database
intro: Your task manager is growing. Users are reporting that loading their task list takes 5+ seconds. Think of it like a library with no catalog — the librarian has to check every shelf to find your book. The database is the bottleneck. How do you fix it without rewriting everything?

teach.title: When Your Database Gets Slow
teach.body: As your data grows, queries that were fast with 100 rows become slow with 100,000 rows. The usual culprits: missing indexes on columns you filter or sort by, queries that fetch more data than needed (SELECT *), and N+1 query problems where your code runs a separate query for each item in a list.

teach.title: Database Indexes Are Like a Book's Index
teach.body: Imagine searching for a word in a 500-page book by reading every page from the start. That's what a database does without an index. Now imagine flipping to the index at the back — it tells you exactly which pages to check. A database index works the same way, skipping straight to the matching rows.

teach.title: Common Database Performance Fixes
teach.code:
SLOW: SELECT * FROM tasks WHERE user_id = 123
  → No index on user_id, scans every row

FAST: CREATE INDEX idx_tasks_user_id ON tasks(user_id);
  → Database jumps directly to matching rows

SLOW: Fetching 10,000 tasks then filtering in JavaScript
FAST: SELECT * FROM tasks WHERE user_id = 123 LIMIT 20 OFFSET 0;
  → Let the database do the filtering and pagination
teach.annotation: Indexes and pagination are the two most impactful database optimizations. They solve 80% of performance problems.

teach.body: A slow database means slow load times, which means users leave. Knowing how to scale your database is the skill that lets your app handle thousands of paying users without breaking a sweat.
Before adding infrastructure, optimize your queries. Add indexes on frequently filtered columns, use pagination, and select only the columns you need. These changes are free and often 10-100x faster.

start.narrative: Users report that loading their task list takes 8 seconds. You check the database and find that the tasks table has 500,000 rows. The query SELECT * FROM tasks WHERE user_id = $1 ORDER BY created_at DESC has no index on user_id. What do you do first?

choices.text: Upgrade to a larger database server with more RAM and CPU
Add an index on the user_id column and add pagination to limit results to 20 per page
Switch to a NoSQL database like MongoDB because SQL can't handle this much data

throw-money.narrative: The bigger server helps temporarily — load times drop to 3 seconds. But the fundamental problem (no index, no pagination) remains. As data grows, you'll need an even bigger server and the costs keep climbing. You finally agree to look at the actual query.

choices.text: Run EXPLAIN ANALYZE on the slow query to see exactly which operation is the bottleneck
Add indexes on every column in the table just to be safe

add-index.narrative: You add an index on user_id and implement pagination (20 tasks per page). Load time drops from 8 seconds to 200ms. But you notice the task detail page is also slow — it loads the task plus all its comments. You check the logs and see 21 separate queries fire for one page load.

choices.text: Confirm with EXPLAIN ANALYZE that this is an N+1 — one query for the task, then one for each comment
Skip diagnosis and assume it's the same indexing problem as before

switch-db.narrative: Migrating to a different database is a massive undertaking — rewriting queries, changing the schema, migrating data. Three weeks later, you discover MongoDB has the same performance issues without indexes. The database type was never the problem. You roll back and finally read EXPLAIN ANALYZE.

choices.text: Run EXPLAIN ANALYZE on the slow query and find the missing index plus an N+1 in the comments fetch
Blame the team for picking the wrong database in the first place

fix-n-plus-one.narrative: The task detail page is firing one query for the task and N more queries — one per comment. With 50 comments that's 51 round-trips to the database for one page. How do you collapse it?

choices.text: Use a single JOIN: SELECT tasks.*, comments.* FROM tasks LEFT JOIN comments ON comments.task_id = tasks.id WHERE tasks.id = $1
Use a DataLoader-style batch: collect all task IDs, then run SELECT * FROM comments WHERE task_id IN (...) once
Keep the loop but parallelize with Promise.all so the queries fire at the same time

add-cache.narrative: The queries are now efficient. Load time is 150ms. But the task list endpoint is hit thousands of times per minute and the database CPU is climbing. How do you take pressure off the database?

choices.text: Add a Redis cache for the task list keyed by user_id, with a 60-second TTL and invalidation on writes
Cache results in an in-process Map inside the Node app
Skip caching — every request should hit the database for accuracy

scale-out.narrative: Caching cut database load by 70%. Traffic keeps growing — your single primary database is now hitting its connection limit and write throughput is maxing out. What's the next move?

choices.text: Add read replicas: send all SELECTs to replicas and keep writes on the primary, then add a connection pooler like PgBouncer
Vertically scale the primary to the largest instance the cloud offers
Shard the tasks table across 16 databases by user_id today

excellent-end.narrative: Your task manager now serves the full traffic load: indexed queries, no N+1s, a cache layer absorbing read pressure, and read replicas plus connection pooling for horizontal headroom. You scaled in the right order — fix the query, then cache, then add hardware.
excellent-end.feedback: This is the textbook scaling order: diagnose with EXPLAIN ANALYZE, fix indexes and N+1s, then cache, then scale out. Skipping the diagnosis or jumping straight to sharding is how teams burn money and ship fragile systems.

mediocre-end.narrative: The bigger box helps for a quarter, then you hit the same ceiling — but now your monthly bill is double what it was and you still haven't separated reads from writes.
mediocre-end.feedback: Vertical scaling buys time but not headroom. Read replicas plus connection pooling cost less and let you keep adding capacity. Reach for them before you outgrow the largest instance.

weak-end.narrative: Sharding without need rewrites every query in the app, breaks transactions across users, and triples on-call complexity. You spend three months on a problem you didn't have yet.
weak-end.feedback: Sharding is a last-resort hammer. Always exhaust indexes, query optimization, caching, and read replicas first. Most apps never need to shard at all.
